sound_type: bark + chatter -- loud sharp alarm bark ('quaa!') followed by rapid chatter of shorter notes, lower-pitched than Douglas Squirrel, less aggressive, scolding conversation rather than territorial assault, E=4, urban park habitat
ecosystem: urban park. Urban parks, college campuses, suburban neighborhoods with mature deciduous and mixed trees. INTRODUCED to PNW -- native to eastern North America. Second urban species after Raccoon (70).
season: year-round. Does NOT hibernate. Active all year. Caches nuts and acorns for winter use.

species_pair.pair_type: Sciuridae SEPTET -- SEVENTH consecutive Sciuridae (75-76-77-78-79-80-81). BREAKS THE TIE with Mustelidae sextet (63-68). LONGEST family run in engine history: SEVEN. Three marmots (Marmota, 75-77) + four squirrels (Urocitellus 78, Callospermophilus 79, Tamiasciurus 80, Sciurus 81). Five genera. Seven habitats. Alpine to urban.

coordinates.description: Reference for urban park habitat in the greater Seattle area. University of Washington campus and surrounding parks where Eastern Fox Squirrels are commonly observed.

taxonomy_note.description: SEVENTH CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE (75-76-77-78-79-80-81). Sciuridae septet. BREAKS Mustelidae tie (63-68). LONGEST family run: SEVEN. NEW GENUS Sciurus -- fifth Sciuridae genus. Twentieth mammalian entry. Eighteenth mammalian species. Eighth Rodentia. Sciuridae's seventh entry. INTRODUCED to PNW.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Sciurus from Greek skiouros ('squirrel,' from skia 'shadow' + oura 'tail'). niger Latin for 'black,' referring to the melanistic morph. Described by Linnaeus (1758).
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Sciurus -- NEW GENUS in the engine. Fifth Sciuridae genus after Marmota (75-77), Urocitellus (78), Callospermophilus (79), Tamiasciurus (80). True squirrels -- the largest genus of tree squirrels.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- eighth Rodentia in the engine.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- twentieth mammalian entry. Eighteenth mammalian species.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's SEVENTH entry at degree 81. Seventh consecutive (75-81). BREAKS the Mustelidae tie (63-68). LONGEST family run: SEVEN.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 1,500-6,000 Hz. Lower-pitched than Douglas Squirrel.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. BARK + CHATTER. Alarm bark followed by rapid chatter.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Bark: 0.1-0.3 seconds. Chatter sequences: 1-5 seconds. Shorter than Douglas Squirrel's sustained rattle.
song_metrics.note_structure: Two-part: initial sharp bark ('quaa!') followed by rapid chatter of shorter notes. Less sustained than Douglas Squirrel.
song_metrics.note_types: Bark (sharp alarm), chatter (rapid sequence), screech (distress), purr (contentment).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Bark-chatter pattern. Less complex than Douglas Squirrel's sustained rattle.
song_metrics.timbre: Lower, less aggressive than Douglas Squirrel. A deeper scolding conversation rather than a machine-gun assault.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Audible across urban parks. Less piercing than Douglas Squirrel at E=5.
song_metrics.tempo: Moderate. Bark-pause-chatter pattern rather than continuous stream.
song_metrics.context: ALARM and TERRITORIAL. Less aggressively territorial than Douglas Squirrel. More generalized alarm response.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal variation in introduced PNW populations.
song_metrics.key_property: BARK + CHATTER -- the 82nd acoustic element. Sharp bark followed by rapid chatter. 1,500-6,000 Hz. E=4. Lower-pitched. Less aggressive. Urban park habitat.

physical_description.overall: Largest tree squirrel in North America. 450-1,000 g (1-2.2 lbs). 45-70 cm total length. Rusty-brown to orange-brown above, pale to orange belly. Variable coloration including melanistic (black) morphs. Much larger than native Douglas Squirrel.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 81: (1) LARGE SIZE -- noticeably larger than Douglas Squirrel, roughly three times the mass. (2) RUSTY-BROWN/ORANGE pelage -- warmer tones than Douglas Squirrel. (3) URBAN PARK habitat -- on the ground in parks, not in conifer canopy. (4) BUSHY TAIL -- large, prominent. (5) BARK + CHATTER vocalization -- deeper, less aggressive than Douglas Squirrel.
physical_description.fur_detail: BODY: Rusty-brown to orange-brown above. Highly variable -- ranges from pale buff to deep russet to melanistic black. BELLY: Pale to orange. TAIL: Very bushy, prominent, often with orange or rust tones.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition. Generalist. Powerful jaws for cracking hard-shelled nuts.
physical_description.field_id: In urban parks: a LARGE squirrel, noticeably bigger than native squirrels, with warm rusty-brown or orange tones, running on the ground or climbing deciduous trees. If the squirrel is big and in a city park, it is likely an Eastern Fox Squirrel.

nodes.title: Sciuridae Septet (75-81) -- BREAKS the Mustelidae Tie! LONGEST Family Run!
nodes.description: Seven consecutive Sciuridae. SURPASSES Mustelidae (63-68). LONGEST family run in engine history: SEVEN. Three marmots + four squirrels. Five genera. Seven habitats. Alpine to urban.

nodes.title: Bark + Chatter -- 82nd Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Sharp alarm bark followed by rapid chatter. 1,500-6,000 Hz. E=4. Lower-pitched than Douglas Squirrel. Less aggressive. Scolding conversation rather than territorial assault.

nodes.title: Urban Park Habitat -- Second Urban Species
nodes.description: Urban parks, campuses, suburban neighborhoods. Second urban species after Raccoon (70). Conifer canopy (80) to urban park (81). The habitat shift reflects the native-introduced dynamic.

nodes.title: Introduced to PNW -- Second Introduced Species
nodes.description: Native to eastern North America. Introduced to PNW urban areas. Second introduced species after Opossum (72). Competes with native Douglas Squirrel.

nodes.title: Largest Tree Squirrel in North America
nodes.description: 450-1,000 g. Three times the mass of Douglas Squirrel. The largest tree squirrel on the continent. Size advantage in competition with natives.

nodes.title: The Sara Bareilles Parallel
nodes.description: Both E=4. Both outsiders who arrived (introduced/Eureka CA). Both larger than life (largest squirrel/Broadway voice). Both thrive in urban/commercial spaces (parks/major labels).

nodes.title: Twenty-First Consecutive Original CSV
nodes.description: Twenty-one consecutive original CSV species. No substitution. CSV reliability continues.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE SCIURIDAE. Native (80) vs. introduced (81). Conifer canopy vs. urban park. Chatter (E=5) vs. bark+chatter (E=4). The native-introduced contrast within the same family run.
URBAN HABITAT SHARED. Both urban-adapted mammals. Raccoon was the first urban species (70). Fox Squirrel is the second (81). Both generalist feeders in human-modified environments.
URBAN HABITAT PARALLEL. Mallard thrives in urban waterways. Fox Squirrel thrives in urban parks. Both common, visible, and successful in human-modified habitats.
INTRODUCED SPECIES PARALLEL. Both INTRODUCED to the PNW. Opossum (72) from eastern/southern North America. Fox Squirrel (81) from eastern North America. Two introductions from the same region.
URBAN PREDATOR-PREY. Red Fox (71) is an urban predator. Eastern Fox Squirrel (81) is urban prey. Both share urban park habitat. The urban food web.
